heroOverview: Uterine fibroids are benign smooth-muscle tumours of the uterine myometrium, driven by oestrogen and progesterone. They are the most common gynaecological tumour in women of reproductive age, present in 70-80% of women by age 50 and causing symptoms in approximately 25-50% of those affected. Heavy menstrual bleeding, pelvic pressure, and pelvic pain are the cardinal symptoms; fibroids are also the leading indication for hysterectomy in the United States. Treatment ranges from observation and hormonal management to minimally invasive uterine artery embolisation and surgical myomectomy, allowing uterus-preserving options for women who wish to maintain fertility.

keyStats.prevalence: Present in 70-80% of women by age 50 (Day Baird et al. Am J Obstet Gynecol 2003); symptomatic in 25-50% of affected women
keyStats.demographics: Black women are affected 2-3× more frequently and at younger ages than white women; fibroids are 2-3× larger and cause more severe symptoms in Black women (Baird et al.); peak incidence age 30-50
keyStats.avgAge: Most commonly diagnosed at age 30-40; onset before age 20 is rare; fibroids typically regress after menopause
keyStats.globalCases: Uterine fibroids account for approximately 200,000 hysterectomies and 30,000 myomectomies annually in the United States

definition: Uterine fibroids (ICD-10: D25, uterine leiomyomata) are benign monoclonal smooth-muscle tumours arising from the myometrium, driven by oestrogen and progesterone receptor activity and dependent on an intact hormonal environment. They are classified by anatomical location relative to the uterine wall: intramural (within the muscle, 70%), submucosal (protruding into the uterine cavity, 5-10%), subserosal (projecting outward from the outer uterine surface, 20%), and pedunculated (on a stalk, subserosal or submucosal). Submucosal fibroids most strongly correlate with heavy bleeding and impaired fertility despite being the least common type. Fibroids range from a few millimetres to more than 20 cm; large fibroids can distort adjacent organs including the bladder, ureters, and rectum. Malignant transformation to leiomyosarcoma is rare — estimated at less than 0.1-0.3% of presumed fibroids — but its clinical distinction from benign leiomyoma pre-operatively is limited, driving conservative surgical approaches. Management is tailored by symptom severity, desire for fertility, fibroid number, size, location, and the patient's preferences. Obstetrics and gynaecology specialists manage fibroids; subspecialists in reproductive medicine address fertility impacts.

typesClassification.description: Develop within the myometrial wall; the most common location (70%). Large intramural fibroids distort the uterine cavity and can cause heavy periods and bulk symptoms even without submucosal extension. Moderate effect on fertility compared with submucosal fibroids.

Protrude into the uterine cavity; strongly associated with heavy menstrual bleeding, abnormal uterine bleeding, and implantation failure or miscarriage. SM0 is completely intracavitary; SM1 has less than 50% intramural extension; SM2 has 50% or more intramural extension. Treated by hysteroscopic myomectomy for SM0 and SM1.

Project outward from the serosal surface; cause predominantly bulk symptoms — pelvic pressure, bladder frequency, urinary urgency, constipation — rather than abnormal bleeding. Very large subserosal fibroids can obstruct ureters.

Attached to the uterus by a stalk; either subserosal (external) or submucosal (internal). Pedunculated subserosal fibroids can torse their pedicle, causing acute severe pelvic pain. Pedunculated submucosal fibroids may prolapse through the cervix.

Arise in the cervical myometrium rather than the uterine body; rare (<1%); can obstruct the cervical canal, impair fertility, or complicate delivery. Surgical access during myomectomy is more difficult than for fundal fibroids.

primarySymptoms: Heavy menstrual bleeding (menorrhagia): soaking through a pad or tampon every hour for several consecutive hours, passing large clots (>2.5 cm), or periods lasting more than 7 days; caused by enlarged endometrial surface area and submucosal fibroid distortion of the cavity.
Pelvic pressure or heaviness: a sensation of fullness, bloating, or weight in the pelvis from uterine enlargement, present even without pain; characteristic of large or multiple fibroids.
Urinary frequency and urgency from anterior fibroid compression of the bladder; in large fibroids, ureteric obstruction can cause hydronephrosis and back pain.
Pelvic pain: dull, crampy, or pressure-like pelvic pain throughout the cycle; acute severe pain from torsion of a pedunculated fibroid or degeneration of a large fibroid during pregnancy.
Dysmenorrhoea: more severe or prolonged menstrual cramping than in women without fibroids, caused by submucosal distortion and prostaglandin release.
Constipation or rectal pressure from posterior or broad-ligament fibroids pressing on the rectum or sigmoid colon.
Abdominal distension: a visibly enlarged or asymmetric lower abdomen in women with large fibroids, sometimes raising concern for ovarian tumour.
Iron-deficiency anaemia from chronic heavy menstrual blood loss; presents with fatigue, pallor, dyspnoea on exertion, and low haemoglobin on blood tests.
Infertility or recurrent miscarriage: submucosal fibroids distort the uterine cavity, impairing implantation; large intramural fibroids decrease natural pregnancy rates.
Back pain: fibroid pressure on the lumbosacral nerve plexus causes lower back or leg pain, particularly with posterior or broad-ligament fibroids.
earlyWarningSigns: Gradual increase in menstrual flow over consecutive cycles with periods becoming progressively heavier and longer than the patient's personal baseline.
New or increasing pelvic pressure or urinary frequency in a woman aged 30-50 without a urological explanation.
Unexplained fatigue and exertional breathlessness in a woman with heavy periods — suggesting iron-deficiency anaemia requiring urgent blood count.
Palpable firm irregular uterine mass on bimanual examination in a woman with recent onset of bulk symptoms or worsening bleeding.
Unexplained infertility or two or more first-trimester miscarriages — triggers investigation including sonohysterography to detect submucosal fibroids.
emergencySigns: Acute severe pelvic pain with a palpable tender uterine mass — possible torsion of a pedunculated fibroid or acute degeneration requiring urgent imaging and surgical review.

Heavy vaginal bleeding with haemodynamic instability (heart rate >100, systolic BP <90), signalling acute haemorrhage requiring emergency assessment and possible uterine artery embolisation or surgery.
Back pain with bilateral leg oedema and raised creatinine in a woman with known fibroids — suspected bilateral ureteric obstruction requiring urgent renal imaging and urology involvement.
Fever, severe uterine tenderness, and raised inflammatory markers in a woman with fibroids — possible degeneration, infection, or pyomyoma requiring urgent imaging and antibiotic therapy.

causes.cause: Oestrogen and progesterone receptor-driven smooth-muscle proliferation
causes.description: Fibroids contain higher concentrations of oestrogen and progesterone receptors than adjacent normal myometrium. Oestrogen stimulates fibroid growth by upregulating growth factors (EGF, IGF-1, TGF-β), while progesterone promotes mitosis and cellular survival. Fibroids grow during high-oestrogen states (reproductive years, pregnancy) and regress after menopause when oestrogen falls.

causes.cause: Somatic mutations in HMGA2, MED12, and other mediator complex genes
causes.description: Each fibroid is monoclonal — arising from a single mutated smooth-muscle cell. MED12 mutations are found in 70% of fibroids and disrupt transcriptional regulation. HMGA2 rearrangements are common in large fibroids. These mutations are somatic (not inherited) and explain why multiple fibroids within the same uterus are clonally distinct.

Early menarche (before age 10), obesity (peripheral oestrogen production in adipose tissue), and nulliparity (absence of the relative anti-oestrogenic effect of full-term pregnancy) create a cumulative oestrogen-dominant environment that promotes fibroid initiation and growth.

Fibroids have an abnormal blood supply with disorganised vessels, prone to infarction and degeneration. Chronic endometrial inflammation and altered local cytokine milieu may promote fibroid initiation in susceptible myometrial progenitor cells.

First-degree relatives of women with fibroids have a 2.5× higher risk of developing them. Hereditary leiomyomatosis and renal cell cancer (HLRCC syndrome) — caused by germline fumarate hydratase mutations — is a rare genetic syndrome causing large aggressive fibroids before age 30 and a 10-15% lifetime risk of renal cell carcinoma.

riskFactors.description: Black women have a 2-3× higher incidence, younger age at onset, more fibroids, larger fibroids, and more severe symptoms than white women. The biological mechanism is multifactorial, including genetic, hormonal, and environmental differences.
Early menarche increases lifetime oestrogen exposure and is associated with a 1.2-1.5× higher risk of fibroid development.
Each full-term pregnancy reduces fibroid risk by approximately 20-30%; the mechanism involves transient involution of the myometrium and alteration in the hormonal environment after delivery.
Adipose tissue converts androgens to oestrogen; obesity is associated with a 1.5-2.5× increased risk of fibroids. Weight reduction reduces peripheral oestrogen production.
A first-degree family history of uterine fibroids confers a 2.5× higher risk. Hereditary syndromes (HLRCC) cause very early-onset fibroids and warrant genetic evaluation.

Vitamin D inhibits fibroid cell proliferation in vitro; women with vitamin D deficiency have a 2-3× higher risk of fibroids in some studies. Supplementation may reduce risk but clinical trials are ongoing.

High red meat consumption is associated with a modestly increased risk (RR ~1.2-1.7) in several cohort studies; green vegetable and fruit intake is inversely associated, though the mechanism is unclear.

affectedDemographics: Women of reproductive age (30-50) carry the greatest burden; fibroids are rare before the first menstrual period and regress after menopause.
Black and African-American women are disproportionately affected, diagnosed at younger ages with larger, more numerous fibroids, and have higher rates of hysterectomy for fibroid-related indications.
Women with early menarche (before age 10) and nulliparous women have elevated risk; childbearing and breastfeeding appear protective.
Women in high-income countries report higher diagnosis rates, likely reflecting greater access to ultrasound screening, but fibroids are a worldwide condition affecting women across all ethnicities.
diagnosisOverview: The diagnostic evaluation begins with a menstrual history, pelvic examination, and targeted questioning about bulk symptoms and fertility wishes. Pelvic ultrasound — transvaginal (preferred for small fibroids) or transabdominal (for large uteri) — is the first-line imaging test, identifying fibroid number, size, location, and blood flow on Doppler. The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) 2021 practice bulletin endorses ultrasound as the initial imaging modality. Saline-infusion sonohysterography (SIS) maps intracavitary extension of submucosal fibroids more accurately than conventional ultrasound and is performed before hysteroscopic myomectomy. MRI of the pelvis provides the most precise fibroid mapping (number, size, location, vascularity, and relationship to the endometrial cavity) and is used before uterine artery embolisation (UAE) or myomectomy to plan the procedure and exclude adenomyosis. MRI is also the best test to differentiate fibroid from adenomyosis — a common coexisting and symptom-mimicking condition. Hysteroscopy directly visualises and can simultaneously treat submucosal fibroids. Complete blood count confirms iron-deficiency anaemia from heavy bleeding. Thyroid function and coagulation studies are obtained when bleeding is unusually severe or the patient is young, to exclude other causes. Endometrial biopsy is indicated when abnormal uterine bleeding occurs in women over 45 or in women with risk factors for endometrial hyperplasia, to exclude malignancy before attributing bleeding to fibroids alone.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging: identifies number, size, and location of fibroids relative to the uterine cavity; assesses blood flow with Doppler
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small ultrasound probe is placed in the vagina for the transvaginal examination (no radiation; 15-20 minutes). Produces better images of small fibroids than abdominal ultrasound. Modest discomfort. Results available immediately from the sonographer.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps submucosal fibroid extent into the uterine cavity precisely; superior to standard ultrasound for pre-hysteroscopic myomectomy planning
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small catheter is passed through the cervix under ultrasound guidance; saline is infused to expand the cavity. Mild cramping during the 10-15 minute procedure. Performed on an outpatient basis without anaesthesia.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Most accurate fibroid mapping for uterine artery embolisation and myomectomy planning; best differentiates fibroid from adenomyosis; assesses degeneration type
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes in the MRI scanner. No radiation; no contrast needed in most cases. Provides precise 3D mapping of all fibroids. Used preferentially before major surgical or interventional procedures.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count (FBC) and iron studies
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies iron-deficiency anaemia from chronic heavy menstrual bleeding; guides pre-operative correction
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw. Haemoglobin, haematocrit, ferritin, and transferrin saturation assess the severity of anaemia. Results returned the same day. Pre-operative anaemia correction reduces transfusion need.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly visualises the uterine cavity; diagnoses and can simultaneously remove submucosal fibroids
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin fibre-optic camera is passed through the cervix into the uterine cavity. Diagnostic hysteroscopy is performed awake or under light sedation; operative hysteroscopic myomectomy is performed under general or regional anaesthesia.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes endometrial hyperplasia and malignancy in women over 45 or with risk factors when attributing bleeding to fibroids
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin catheter is passed through the cervix to sample the endometrial lining. Takes 1-2 minutes in the office; cramping for a few minutes afterward. Results available in 3-5 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment selection is driven by symptom severity, fibroid size and location, desire for future pregnancy, proximity to menopause, and patient preference. Watchful waiting is appropriate for asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic fibroids. Medical management targets heavy bleeding and pain: combined oral contraceptives, progestogens, the levonorgestrel intrauterine system (LNG-IUS), and NSAIDs are first-line agents. GnRH agonists (leuprolide, nafarelin) reduce fibroid volume by 30-60% within 3 months through oestrogen suppression but cause menopausal side effects with long-term use; they are most commonly used for 3 months to reduce fibroid and uterine volume before surgery, improving haemoglobin levels. GnRH antagonists (elagolix, relugolix) with hormonal add-back therapy are FDA-approved for managing fibroid-related heavy bleeding and are an effective long-term medical option. Selective progesterone receptor modulators (ulipristal acetate) were used in Europe but were suspended due to rare hepatotoxicity. Uterine artery embolisation (UAE) is a radiological procedure occluding the uterine arteries supplying the fibroids, achieving symptom improvement in 75-85% of patients and preserving the uterus. Myomectomy — surgical removal of fibroids while preserving the uterus — is the standard intervention for women wishing to maintain fertility; it can be performed hysteroscopically (submucosal fibroids), laparoscopically, or by open laparotomy. Hysterectomy is definitive and eliminates recurrence; it remains the most performed surgical treatment for fibroids in the US. ACOG 2021 guidelines support all options and emphasise shared decision-making.

medicalTreatments.name: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system (LNG-IUS, Mirena 52 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: A T-shaped device placed in the uterine cavity releases 20 µg levonorgestrel daily, inducing endometrial atrophy and reducing menstrual blood loss. Most effective for smaller fibroids that do not distort the cavity; may be expelled by large submucosal fibroids.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces menstrual blood loss by 70-90% within 6 months in women without significant cavity distortion; comparable to surgical alternatives in mild-moderate fibroid-related bleeding

medicalTreatments.name: Combined oral contraceptive pill (OCP)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces dysmenorrhoea and moderately decreases menstrual blood loss by suppressing endometrial proliferation. Does not reduce fibroid size. Suitable for women who also want contraception.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 30-50% reduction in menstrual blood loss; modest effect on pelvic pain; does not alter fibroid natural history

medicalTreatments.name: GnRH agonist (leuprolide acetate 3.75 mg/month IM or nafarelin nasal)
medicalTreatments.description: Creates a temporary hypo-oestrogenic state, reducing fibroid and uterine volume by 30-60% within 3 months. Used for 3 months pre-operatively to improve anaemia, reduce fibroid volume, and facilitate minimally invasive surgery. Long-term use causes bone loss.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 30-60% fibroid volume reduction at 3 months; rapid bleeding cessation; bone density loss limits use to 3-6 months without add-back therapy

medicalTreatments.name: Relugolix combination (relugolix 40 mg + estradiol 1 mg + norethindrone 0.5 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral GnRH antagonist plus hormonal add-back; approved by the FDA in 2021 for fibroid-related heavy menstrual bleeding. Achieves rapid bleeding control (within 1-2 months) without the initial flare associated with GnRH agonists. Can be used for up to 24 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 73% of women achieved clinically meaningful bleeding reduction versus 19% placebo at 24 weeks in phase-3 LIBERTY trials

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (mefenamic acid 500 mg three times daily or ibuprofen 400-800 mg three times daily during menses)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduce prostaglandin-driven dysmenorrhoea and provide moderate reduction in menstrual blood loss. Used as adjunctive treatment alongside hormonal therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 25-35% reduction in menstrual blood loss; significant dysmenorrhoea relief in most patients

surgicalOptions.name: Hysteroscopic myomectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Resection of submucosal fibroids through the cervix using a resectoscope or morcellator. The gold-standard for SM0 and SM1 submucosal fibroids. Preserves the uterus, eliminates the fibroid, and restores the uterine cavity for fertility.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 80-90% resolution of heavy bleeding; up to 50-60% improvement in fertility outcomes; recurrence requiring re-operation in 10-15% at 5 years

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic myomectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Keyhole removal of intramural and subserosal fibroids using laparoscopic instruments. Reduces hospital stay and recovery compared with open myomectomy. Preferred for 1-4 fibroids less than 8-10 cm accessible by laparoscopy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 75-85% symptom relief; recurrence of symptomatic fibroids requiring further intervention in 20-25% at 5 years; uterine rupture risk in subsequent pregnancy approximately 0.5-1%

surgicalOptions.name: Open (abdominal) myomectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of multiple or very large fibroids through a low transverse or vertical abdominal incision. Allows the most complete fibroid removal and uterine repair. Preferred when fibroids are numerous, very large, or deeply intramural.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 80-90% symptom relief; fibroid recurrence requiring surgery in 25-30% at 5 years; uterine rupture risk in subsequent pregnancy approximately 0.5-1%; recovery 4-6 weeks

surgicalOptions.name: Uterine artery embolisation (UAE/UFE)
surgicalOptions.description: Interventional radiology procedure: small polyvinyl alcohol particles are injected through a femoral artery catheter to occlude both uterine arteries, depriving fibroids of blood supply and causing infarction and shrinkage over 3-6 months. Uterus is preserved. Not recommended for women actively seeking pregnancy due to ovarian reserve impact.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 75-85% significant symptom improvement at 1 year; uterine volume reduction of 30-50%; 20-25% require additional intervention (hysterectomy or repeat UAE) at 5 years

surgicalOptions.name: Hysterectomy (total or subtotal)
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of the uterus; the only definitive treatment with no risk of fibroid recurrence. Total hysterectomy removes the uterus and cervix; subtotal preserves the cervix. Can be performed laparoscopically, laparoscopic-assisted vaginally, vaginally, or by open laparotomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 100% cure of fibroid symptoms; no recurrence; 5-year patient satisfaction above 95%; recovery 2-6 weeks depending on approach

alternativeTreatments.name: Focused ultrasound surgery (MRI-guided — MRgFUS or ExAblate)
alternativeTreatments.description: High-intensity focused ultrasound energy is delivered to fibroid tissue under real-time MRI guidance, causing thermal coagulation and fibroid necrosis without surgery. FDA-approved; suitable for single or few fibroids in women who decline surgery or embolisation. Maintains fertility in treated women in limited data.

alternativeTreatments.name: Endometrial ablation
alternativeTreatments.description: Destroys the endometrial lining to control heavy bleeding; appropriate for women who have completed childbearing and have no significant submucosal fibroids. Combined with myomectomy for selected women with both submucosal fibroids and diffuse endometrial bleeding.

whySeeSpecialist: An obstetrician-gynaecologist should evaluate any woman with heavy menstrual bleeding affecting quality of life, pelvic bulk symptoms limiting daily activity, or infertility with a suspected uterine cause. A reproductive endocrinology and infertility (REI) subspecialist should be involved when fibroids and fertility impairment coexist. An interventional radiologist is required for uterine artery embolisation.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a gynaecologist experienced in minimally invasive surgery (laparoscopic and hysteroscopic myomectomy) if fertility preservation is a priority. If you are considering UAE, confirm the interventional radiologist has a dedicated women's health or fibroid programme with post-procedure gynaecology follow-up. For very large or numerous fibroids, a high-volume academic centre with all treatment options reduces the risk of conversion to open surgery.
hospitalCriteria: Minimally invasive gynaecological surgery programme with laparoscopic and hysteroscopic myomectomy capability
Interventional radiology service offering uterine artery embolisation with same-day discharge protocol
Reproductive medicine consultation for women with fibroids and infertility
Haematology support for pre-operative iron infusion in women with severe fibroid-related anaemia
MRI availability for pre-operative fibroid mapping and differential diagnosis from adenomyosis
keyFacilities: Academic gynaecology centres with all uterine-sparing treatment options
High-volume fibroid centres with fibroid-specific multidisciplinary teams
Interventional radiology centres with women's health vascular programmes
Outpatient hysteroscopy suites for small submucosal fibroid removal
preventionStrategies: Maintain a healthy body weight — BMI under 25 reduces peripheral oestrogen production from adipose tissue and lowers fibroid risk.
Increase intake of green vegetables and fruits, which are inversely associated with fibroid risk in cohort studies.
Achieve adequate vitamin D status — serum 25-OH vitamin D above 30 ng/mL is associated with lower fibroid incidence in observational studies.
Avoid prolonged use of exogenous oestrogen-only preparations, which drive fibroid growth, and prefer progesterone-containing formulations when contraception is needed.
Seek timely diagnosis and treatment of early-stage or mildly symptomatic fibroids — treatment of small fibroids is simpler with fewer complications than waiting for large symptomatic growth.
lifestyleModifications: Adopt an anti-inflammatory diet: reduce red meat, increase green leafy vegetables, legumes, and oily fish, which reduce systemic inflammation and oestrogen-driven fibroid stimulation.
Exercise regularly — moderate aerobic exercise (150 minutes per week) is associated with a lower risk of symptomatic fibroid disease and supports weight management.
Track menstrual bleeding using a pictorial blood assessment chart or a period-tracking app to quantify volume changes objectively and communicate them to your clinician.
Manage iron-deficiency anaemia with dietary iron (red meat in moderation, beans, fortified cereals) and oral iron supplements as directed before and after surgery.
Limit alcohol consumption — alcohol raises oestrogen levels and is modestly associated with increased fibroid risk.
Discuss all current medications and supplements with your gynaecologist; some phytoestrogens and herbal supplements may stimulate fibroid growth.

recommended: Green vegetables (broccoli, kale, spinach) and legumes — inversely associated with fibroid risk in multiple cohort studies
Iron-rich foods (fortified cereals, lentils, lean meat) and vitamin C to correct anaemia from heavy periods
Dairy products — associated with modestly lower fibroid risk in Nurses' Health Study data (RR 0.8)
Vitamin D-rich or fortified foods (fatty fish, fortified milk) to maintain adequate vitamin D status
avoid: High red and processed meat intake — positively associated with fibroid risk (RR 1.2-1.7) in cohort data
Excess alcohol — raises circulating oestradiol and is associated with fibroid growth
Excess refined sugar and ultra-processed foods, which promote insulin resistance and weight gain, indirectly elevating oestrogen
Soy products in large amounts (more than 2 servings/day) — phytoestrogens may stimulate fibroid growth in some patients, though evidence is inconsistent

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic exercise (brisk walking, swimming, cycling) for at least 150 minutes per week is associated with lower fibroid incidence and symptom severity. During heavy menstrual bleeding, light activity is appropriate; strenuous exercise during acute pelvic pain should be avoided. After UAE, light walking resumes within days but strenuous activity is restricted for 1-2 weeks. After laparoscopic myomectomy, light activity resumes at 1-2 weeks, full activity at 4-6 weeks. After open myomectomy, return to exercise takes 6-8 weeks.
dailyManagement: Use a pictorial blood assessment chart to quantify menstrual flow and identify trends over months.
Take prescribed iron supplements with vitamin C on an empty stomach or as directed to correct anaemia before surgery.
Apply heat pads or TENS to the lower abdomen during dysmenorrhoea for symptomatic relief.
Keep track of urinary symptoms and bowel changes, which may signal fibroid growth pressing on adjacent organs.
Attend regular 6-12 monthly ultrasound reviews if fibroids are managed conservatively, to monitor growth.
Contact your gynaecologist promptly if there is a sudden change in symptoms — new acute pain, sudden increase in bleeding, or a fever.
prognosis: The outlook for women with uterine fibroids is generally excellent with modern treatment. Most asymptomatic fibroids require no treatment and regress naturally after menopause. Symptomatic fibroids respond to medical or surgical treatment in the vast majority of women, with patient satisfaction above 90% after definitive surgical options. Hysterectomy is curative with no recurrence. Myomectomy has fibroid recurrence rates of 20-30% at 5 years — higher with multiple fibroids — but re-operation rates are substantially lower. UAE has equivalent patient satisfaction to myomectomy at 1 year with fewer short-term complications, though 20-25% of women require additional intervention within 5 years. For women with fibroids and infertility, myomectomy of submucosal fibroids increases live birth rates by approximately 50-60% in some series. Women with very large or numerous fibroids have higher surgical complication rates and longer recovery. Leiomyosarcoma occurs in less than 0.3% of women operated on for presumed fibroids, but prognosis is poor when it occurs.
recoveryTimeline: Hysteroscopic myomectomy: same-day or overnight, return to work 1-3 days. Laparoscopic myomectomy: 1-2 nights hospital, return to work 2-3 weeks, pelvic rest 4-6 weeks. Open myomectomy: 2-4 nights, return to work 4-6 weeks. UAE: 1 night hospital, return to work 1-2 weeks; fibroid shrinkage over 3-6 months. Hysterectomy: laparoscopic 1-3 nights and return at 2-4 weeks; open 3-5 nights and return at 6 weeks.
complications: Iron-deficiency anaemia from heavy chronic menstrual blood loss — haemoglobin below 80 g/L warrants IV iron or blood transfusion before elective surgery.
Infertility and recurrent miscarriage from submucosal fibroid cavity distortion — detected and treated by hysteroscopic myomectomy.
Fibroid degeneration during pregnancy: acute red degeneration causes severe localised uterine pain; managed with analgesics and monitoring in most cases.
Ureteric obstruction from large lateral fibroids causing hydronephrosis and progressive renal impairment — requires urgent imaging and urological involvement.
Uterine rupture in subsequent pregnancy following myomectomy: risk approximately 0.5-1%; higher with deep intramural excision; requires caesarean section planning.
Post-UAE expulsion of a necrotic fibroid through the cervix — occurs in 3-5% and may require hysteroscopic extraction or, rarely, hysterectomy.

supportResources.name: The White Dress Project — Fibroids Awareness
supportResources.description: Patient-led advocacy organisation for fibroid awareness, particularly focused on the disproportionate impact on Black women.

supportResources.name: ACOG — Uterine Fibroids Patient FAQ
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information from the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists on fibroid diagnosis and treatment options.

supportResources.name: Society of Interventional Radiology — UFE Patient Information
supportResources.description: Detailed patient guide to uterine fibroid embolisation from the leading US interventional radiology society.

confusedWithConditions.name: Endometriosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Endometriosis and fibroids both cause heavy periods and pelvic pain but differ in mechanism and management. Endometriosis involves endometrial-type tissue implanted outside the uterus, causing inflammation, adhesions, and deep infiltrating nodules. Fibroids are benign smooth-muscle tumours within the myometrium. MRI reliably distinguishes them; the two conditions frequently coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PCOS causes irregular periods and menorrhagia from anovulation rather than a uterine structural abnormality. PCOS is associated with insulin resistance, hyperandrogenaemia, and polycystic ovaries on ultrasound. Fibroids cause heavy regular or irregular bleeding from uterine masses visible on ultrasound. The conditions can coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PID presents with fever, abnormal vaginal discharge, and acute pelvic tenderness from ascending genital tract infection. Fibroids present with heavy bleeding, pressure, and pelvic mass without fever or acute infection. Both may cause pelvic pain; PID requires antibiotics while fibroids require hormonal or surgical management.

relatedConditions.name: Endometriosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-occurs with fibroids in 6-15% of women; both cause heavy periods and pelvic pain, managed by the same specialty and sometimes at the same surgical sitting.

relatedConditions.name: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Both present with pelvic pain in women of reproductive age; important to distinguish infection from structural fibroid disease before treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Both PCOS and fibroids cause heavy or irregular periods in women of reproductive age; they can coexist and both require hormonal management.

relatedConditions.name: Iron-Deficiency Anaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Heavy fibroid-related bleeding is one of the most common causes of iron-deficiency anaemia in premenopausal women; correcting anaemia is an essential component of fibroid management.

faqs.question: What are uterine fibroids?
faqs.answer: Uterine fibroids are benign (non-cancerous) smooth-muscle tumours that grow in or around the uterus. They are the most common gynaecological tumour in women, present in 70-80% of women by age 50. Most cause no symptoms; when symptomatic, heavy periods, pelvic pressure, and pain are most common.

faqs.question: What symptoms do uterine fibroids cause?
faqs.answer: Heavy or prolonged menstrual bleeding is the most common symptom. Others include pelvic pressure or pain, urinary frequency, constipation, abdominal bloating, and back pain. Submucosal fibroids can also impair fertility or cause recurrent miscarriage. About 25-50% of women with fibroids experience significant symptoms.

faqs.question: Are uterine fibroids cancerous?
faqs.answer: Uterine fibroids are benign and not cancerous. Malignant transformation to leiomyosarcoma is estimated to occur in fewer than 0.3% of women operated on for presumed fibroids. Rapid fibroid growth or new pelvic mass in a postmenopausal woman warrants prompt evaluation to exclude malignancy.

faqs.question: Can fibroids cause infertility?
faqs.answer: Yes, submucosal fibroids distort the uterine cavity and significantly impair implantation, reducing natural conception rates and increasing miscarriage risk. Large intramural fibroids also reduce fertility. Hysteroscopic myomectomy for submucosal fibroids improves live birth rates by approximately 50-60% in affected women.

faqs.question: How are uterine fibroids diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Pelvic ultrasound is the first-line test. Transvaginal ultrasound identifies fibroid number, size, and location relative to the uterine cavity. MRI provides the most precise mapping and distinguishes fibroids from adenomyosis. Saline infusion sonohysterography maps submucosal fibroid extension before hysteroscopic surgery.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for uterine fibroids?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on symptoms, fibroid location, fertility wishes, and proximity to menopause. The LNG-IUS (Mirena) controls heavy bleeding in women without cavity distortion. Myomectomy removes fibroids while preserving the uterus. UAE is a minimally invasive alternative. Hysterectomy is definitive for women who have completed childbearing.

faqs.question: Can fibroids shrink on their own?
faqs.answer: Fibroids naturally regress after menopause when oestrogen levels fall. GnRH agonists and antagonists can temporarily shrink fibroids by 30-60%, but regrowth occurs when treatment stops. There is no proven medical treatment that permanently reduces fibroid size without surgery or embolisation.

faqs.question: What is uterine artery embolisation (UAE)?
faqs.answer: UAE is a minimally invasive procedure in which an interventional radiologist injects tiny particles through a catheter in the femoral artery to block blood supply to fibroids. Fibroids shrink by 30-50% over 3-6 months. Symptoms improve in 75-85% of women; the uterus is preserved.

faqs.question: Does myomectomy cure fibroids?
faqs.answer: Myomectomy removes existing fibroids and relieves symptoms in 80-90% of women. However, new fibroids can develop after myomectomy, with recurrence rates of 20-30% at 5 years. Women with multiple fibroids have higher recurrence rates. Hysterectomy is the only option with zero recurrence risk.

faqs.question: Can I get pregnant after fibroid surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes. Myomectomy preserves the uterus and is the standard fertility-preserving operation. Pregnancy after myomectomy is generally safe, though uterine rupture risk is approximately 0.5-1%, which typically requires caesarean delivery. UAE is generally not recommended for women who wish to conceive due to possible ovarian reserve impact.

faqs.question: Why are fibroids more common in Black women?
faqs.answer: Black women have a 2-3× higher incidence of fibroids, develop them at younger ages, and have larger and more numerous fibroids than white women. Contributing factors include genetic differences in fibroid biology, higher rates of vitamin D deficiency, earlier menarche, and possibly dietary and environmental exposures.

faqs.question: What causes heavy periods with fibroids?
faqs.answer: Fibroids increase the surface area of the endometrial lining, impairing normal haemostasis. Submucosal fibroids distort the uterine cavity most directly. Fibroids also alter uterine contractility and increase local prostaglandin levels, both of which worsen menstrual blood loss.

faqs.question: Do fibroids get worse with age?
faqs.answer: Fibroids typically grow during reproductive years when oestrogen is high. Growth is fastest in the thirties and forties. After menopause, oestrogen levels fall and most fibroids regress substantially. Hormone replacement therapy after menopause can maintain fibroid size or stimulate regrowth.

faqs.question: What are the signs that a fibroid needs surgery?
faqs.answer: Surgery is considered when heavy bleeding causes anaemia, when bulk symptoms significantly impair quality of life, when fertility is affected, when urinary obstruction develops, or when fibroids grow rapidly in a postmenopausal woman raising concern for malignancy.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a fibroid and a cyst?
faqs.answer: A fibroid is a solid benign smooth-muscle tumour of the uterine wall. An ovarian cyst is a fluid-filled sac in or on the ovary. Both can cause pelvic pain and pressure, but they are different structures managed differently. Ultrasound readily distinguishes them.

metaTitle: Uterine Fibroids: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Uterine fibroids affect 70-80% of women by 50 causing heavy periods and pelvic pain. Diagnosis, hormonal treatment, myomectomy and UAE options reviewed here.
